speaker: interviewer
text: Take 2 of an interview with Rachel Kessler on July 29th, 1996. Mrs. Kessler, we were talking about your job at Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer. How did you become such a linguist?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just, I just studied. I would buy a grammar book, a regular study book. And I would make sure I got the pronunciation correct. And then I would start learning it. And I would repeat the sentences. Especially the foreign language sentences, I would repeat until I got them really smooth. And then I just did. Oh, then, when I'd done sufficiently, then I would buy a novel. I would read a novel, just to read it quickly, too, to get a feeling for the shapes of the sentences, you know. They vary in different languages. Then I would find somebody who wanted to practice English. And I would meet with them twice during the lunch hour during the week. And for half an hour, we would speak English for the other person to practice. And for half an hour, we would practice my language.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that time, you were speaking to your husband in French?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in contact with your family in England while you were in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, oh yes. We wrote letters. And every-- well, several times during that time I visited.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they starting to worry about war? Were you starting to feel like the atmosphere was charged?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1939, yes. Now in 1939, my husband had obtained a job with Vickers-Armstrong in England. And we were going to be able to go and live in England. He had once before had a job in London, a company there had engaged him as a particular kind of specialist that they needed. And so he had to obtain a visa for that. Among the papers as I was looking, I found one that said that he wasn't allowed to take any other kind of work, something where they were covering, because England was not allowing foreigners to come and take jobs in England. So those, the actual-- what would you call the workshop, the man in charge of the workshop was very jealous of Martin. And he told the boss, we don't need any foreign experts here, we'll do it on our own. And it was so unpleasant that it was decided that Martin wouldn't stay. So we couldn't stay. But eventually he got this job at Vickers-Armstrong where we would have been able to stay. But soon the war broke out. And when things were going bad in France, the trains in the south of France had already been stopped, so there was no-- no trains going. I said to my boss, I think I'd better go to England with my daughter and join Martin there. So I did. And the very next day, we had to come back. They would have liked to keep-- Vickers-Armstrong would have liked to keep him because he was very useful in manufacturing the-- it was called the Wellington or some kind of plane. But so he was saved from actively being engaged in a plane that would have gone to kill people, you know. That's a point-of-view too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his company got in touch with the French Embassy in London-- what Martin was supposed to do. They needed him, but he's a French citizen. And they said he had to go back to France and join his company. So we went back and he joined. And they sent him back home because they hadn't decided what to do with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then about a week or so later then, they-- he received his consignment and he was to be a liaison with the British Army. And he was-- he went to the north of France and was with a group of colonels and captains of the British anti-aircraft.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you while he was away in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was not very comfortable because I-- well, first of all, the first year, I sent my daughter away because we expected Paris to be bombed. So the top boss at Metro-Goldwyn had a place out in the country, had a horse farm in Normandy. And so I took him there and left him there with them for a while. Then neither of us was happy and nothing was happening in Paris. So I went back and brought her back home and she went to school. Now there were plenty of alerts. In the middle of the night, there would be screaming of alerts. And we would get up and go down into the cellar of the house-- it was a big house with a number of apartments-- until the all clear went. And that happened very often. But there was only one bombing of Paris. And that was a distance away from us. And that did happen when my little girl was in school. And so I run down-- ran down to the school, and the children, the school children were still walking up from the subway station where they had been sent during the alert. And I think she was the last one. And I just waited and waited for her. And so that gives a little of the atmosphere.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- what happened when your husband left and went to-- well, let's go back to when war broke out. What was that like when the Germans invaded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they invaded Belgium first. But early in June when we had heard that they had come near to Paris, and there were some right near Paris, friends had just bought a new car and they were sure the German-- they were not Jewish, but they were sure the Germans would take it away. And they wanted us to drive it away. Martin was already gone because the Army had released him finally to a British company that wanted him to work with them. And they had gone. So I couldn't drive in those days. And we got together, two families, and they were driving to Spain, to drive that, that corner, the southwest corner of France where people were refugeeing to get into Spain. And-- but Martin and his family were-- and his group, his office, were on the river Loire in a place, little place called [INAUDIBLE]. And we drove there. And this other man left us there and he drove off with the car. So I was with Mark. We slept on the floor that night. And then we, we heard the Germans were coming. The French were not going after all to set up a line there. They were going to go further south, maybe to North Africa. And so we stood on the, on the platform all day until late at night, one train after train full of refugees, even things piled over on top of, top of the train, would pass by and not stop, till finally one stopped. And we squeezed in. There was not much room, and we just squeezed in. I forget what I had. I think I had grabbed a package of dried fruit I would give my child to suck every now and then one. We got there, instead of getting there at midnight, to, to Bordeaux, we arrived at noon the next day. Martin's office was set up, but we found a place on the outskirts of Bordeaux in a little-- we slept overnight in a little wooden hut on the land of a market gardener. And that night, we heard bang, big bangs and noise. And the Germans were bombing Bordeaux that night. We stood out, we stood outside the hut. We could see the flashes in the distance. Then we knew we had to get away because the Germans were arriving. They were closing that corner. For a while there, that had been pretty free. And Martin, Martin offered the market gardener some amount of money to take us away from there. Well, he wouldn't accept all of it. He took some, but he wouldn't accept all. He was a decent man. And how we went-- so he drove us away from there and we landed adventures in a little village where we stayed overnight in a house with all the windows broken. It was an abandoned house. We slept on the floor there. And the next morning, a man of the village, an anti-Semite, very angrily-- Martin remembers this better than I do-- ordered us out of there, they didn't want any Jews in their village. This was France, but he was an anti-Semite. There was a farmer woman who'd heard this. And she took care of us and took us away to, took us to a, an abandoned farm house where we stayed overnight. And then we found, well, there's nothing we could do there because we had no money. And we decided-- and any money we had, any house we had was in Paris. We decided we'd have to go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we began going back, and Martin got police to sign that he was no longer in the army, demobilized him. Back in Paris we were, and how are we going to get away now? We have really have to find some way because things were getting bad. We were under the Germans actually for a few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then somebody told Martin a way he could go. You take a certain road and go through a certain forest up to a certain point, and you'll be beyond the demarcation line. And so he said goodbye to us and he was to send me a postcard saying, whatever it was, that I should go the same way he he'd gone, with a little girl. Well, on the way in the forest, he was with a young man who had joined him. And suddenly they heard a noise. And Martin said to this young man, stand still, don't budge, just stand still. But he got frightened and he began to run. Well, they shot him dead and Martin had to carry his dead body back to the [INAUDIBLE], the German police station where, where they roughed him up and took his shoes away. And his papers were given to an officer there to to look at. The officer found among the papers one which showed that Martin had been in the Austro-Hungarian Army. And this man said, you know, I'm not a Nazi. So listen, here are you shoes. And you watch the door at the back there. A train is going to pass by there in a few minutes. You just run out quickly and jump in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came back to Paris, a knock at the door and then back is Martin. So then he didn't dare stay because they had his name and address. So he found some other way to go. And this time, he got through. And he was to send us information from Marseille, a card saying so and so, that it was safe for me to go that way too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a friend, Suzanne, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who, to whom I told, told this, that I was going to go with Ann, and my Annie, my little girl. And we were going to go the same way. And she said, don't do that. It's too dangerous, don't do that. Now I don't know whether Martin has told you this story or mentioned Suzanne or told you who Suzanne was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Suzanne's last name, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we knew her as a girl, it was Suzanne DeBreuker. But her married name-- it will have to come, it may come back to me. She was married to the man, when she married him, he was a youth leader in Germany. But he's the man who had been put in charge in France, he was the chief German in France. And Suzanne was his wife there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she came to see me. And actually I have in my documents a birthday letter she sent to Ann in that December. So she said, don't do that, just wait, just wait. Wait, wait a few days, wait a few days. So I waited. And in the meantime, Martin didn't know what was going on and he thought we'd been caught. He saw some article in a newspaper about a woman with a child. And he was on the point of coming back to Paris. And, but she, she gave me, she got me a paper, a permit. And she got me a permit to take the train across the demarcation line into the south of France. And so we did and we arrived. And-- What was that like in Paris waiting? Where were you waiting in the-- were you in an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris, we had an apartment, in an apartment building. There was maybe eight or 10 apartments in the whole building.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like waiting and not knowing where he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a kind of anguish, I suppose. You know, it's a kind of anguish. You've still got to do your job. I had my job to go to, my little girl to go to school. And you just wait and you just wait and wait. Especially when he came down back home again, that was really a shock. But eventually things worked out and we were together in Marseille.

speaker: interviewer
text: And What happened in Marseille?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went straight to the Metro-Goldwyn office in Marseille. And from there, I was conducting secret correspondence with the Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer in Paris. We had a runner who would take correspondence back and forth. So I was running things from Marseille. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we tried-- how are we going to get out. Martin went to [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE] said, because he thought they would help us to go to Israel from there. We had to get somewhere. And they said no. For Israel, they were only interested in workman, people who could work the land and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: He could have done it, if he had to because he is quite strong. He used to be quite strong. But still, he was an educated man. And that wasn't the kind that they wanted to send to Israel. Strange isn't it? So he went to the Spanish Embassy and he dropped that because it was such a long line waiting to go through Spain. So I received the papers from my sister, and I got a seat aboard the [INAUDIBLE] boat that was going to the Martinique. I could get as far as the Martinique, and then from the Martinique, somehow get-- but at least I would be getting away from there. The Martinique is still considered as a province of France. So what am I going to do about my husband? You know, well, a couple of things happened. I happened to know-- and I've forgotten the girls name-- terrible that I've forgotten it. I met a girl once in Paris, just met her. I happened to be with somebody and we met this young woman. And I was introduced and there wasn't even much conversation. I just, just met this girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, by one of the miracles, she was in-- with the French government, in one of the government offices in-- oh, where was it-- where the French government was set up. So I typed up a letter saying, that this Martin Kessler needed to go to the, to, to the Martinique on business, which was really ridiculous. Nobody was doing business in February 1940, '41. But it looked OK on paper. And the manager of the Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer of Marseilles was nice enough to sign it. And I sent that to Vichy because I had heard somehow that this girl was in a certain office in Vichy until she received it. And so to say, I think she even said that, at some point, put on her hat and walked across to the, to the, to the what ministry-- what ministry, and asked someone to, oh, just put a stamp on this for me. And they put the stamp on it and she sent it back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had a permit to go on business to Martinique. But how was he going to go? Now my boss, the top boss at Metro-Goldwyn owned, owned the horse farm in Normandy. Suddenly he turned up in Marseille. He was in New York, but he flew over. I mean, this dangerous thing because the Germans might have run over, run just then, when I think about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave me a lot of money, I think it was a million francs. That scared me, and I locked it up. And the next day, I was to give it to a man who was coming who was bringing from his horse farm, bringing over the demarcation line, 40 mares that were about to foal. And I was to meet this man, which I did. And then I'd done that job, that was all. So I went to see the head of the French line on which the boat I was taking on the Monday and I went on a Saturday afternoon. And I talked to him. And I said, you know, my boss has all these horses. I doubt if he'll leave them here. He'll want to send them somewhere else. Does the transatlantic line-- that's what he was, he was the head of the French transit-- have facilities on ships to take them to North Africa, because North Africa had areas for horses and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, oh yes, we could do that. I said, well, OK, now I'm going to see him in New York, I'm leaving Monday. Supposing he would rather have the ship sent to America or South America or somewhere, can you still do that? Yes, we can do that, he said. Oh good. So I'll know to tell him that. By the way, I'm taking this ship on Monday, you wouldn't have a room there for my my husband? And he said, just a minute, and he inquired. Yes, there was just one space left on that ship. So Martin came on that ship and he was in a cabin with several French officers who were coming to this country to be on the Normandy that was in New York City, in the harbor. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm losing my thread. That ship, he joined you in Martinique.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so we got to Martinique. Before we arrived in the Martinique, we were boarded by French officers who took away any money we had with us and separated the men from the women. And then when we arrived, we were sent to different camps. At the, at the first day, they put us through the examining as though we were regular travelers-- customs, we were examined at customs. And I had a pile of folders, I brought a lot of papers with me. The things that I brought from France for, for Metro-Goldwyn. And so they-- I got a man and he would take one folder up and open it and look through it and close it and put it on one side. And he would be constantly taking a folder up and put it down on the other pile. At one point, he was called away for something. And while he was gone, I thought to myself, do I, do I or don't I, because I could see that the next folder had information in it about the horse farm that they, that the Germans had seized. And so I just quickly took that and turned it over. And also, I knew the name of the Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer-- the man in the Metro-- Martinique man who did business with Metro-Goldwyn. And I said to Martin, just go quickly and find a telephone and telephone to him, which he did. But he didn't get him. And I have a letter among the things from this Didier, his name was, that he'd-- for two days, he hadn't got the message. When he did get the message, he was a man of influence there, and he came and got us out. I, I from, me from one camp, Martin from another camp. And I have a photograph among the photographs that I got out there of a little cottage in the hills that he had. And he put us in his little cottage as long as we needed to stay there. And Martin went down into town and saw the American attache and sent-- oh, he, Martin, by a miracle-- you like the word miracle again. Martin had a Hungarian number, an immigration number, which was very unusual because, according to the law that was passed here somewhere in the 1920s, there were only so many they could-- there were a lot of numbers available for British people like me, so to say, because it didn't matter where you, where you were born. But he was born in Hungary. And by a miracle, he had a number because the British officers had sent him on errands sometimes. And once he came-- had to come through Paris, and he was walking, suddenly he realized he was walking past the American Embassy. And he thought, oh, hmm, so he walked in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, I, I want a number for a visa. And they laughed and they laughed. What do you mean? You, you never, it'd take you I don't know how many years before your number would come up. Never mind, he said, never mind, give me one just the same. So he had this number. So the [FRENCH] in Martinique was able to put all this on a telegram and we got OK. So he was able to arrive. Because at that point in the war, I don't know whether Hungary had just declared war on the United States or something. It was just about that time Hungary was involved in it. So we did arrive. When you say camps, what kind of camps were you in in Martinique?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a military camp. Would be like a big building with, with rooms, corridors and rooms. Very simple wood, very simple beds and so on. Right, in the next room to where I was, there was a man of the Martinique who had been jailed by the officials because they caught him listening on the radio to de Gaulle, because it was forbidden to listen to de Gaulle on the radio. So they caught him and they put him in there for awhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jews also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I guess most of us would be Jews who were coming. There was a Turkish couple. And when they'd taken us off the boat, they put us, put the women in a, in a bus. And they were going to drive it. And this woman, she was crying so bitterly. She was really bitterly crying. And I comforted her. I gave her a candy to suck. I had some candies and I said, here, here, suck this. And so she sucked the candy. And she-- I guess she felt comfort in that somebody was sympathizing with her or whatever. So I don't know. That doesn't answer any question.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how did you manage to then get on a boat and leave? Or when did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean from the Martinique? We had our, we had, I had, I had visas for myself and my daughter. And Martin got his visa through the [FRENCH]. And then after a while, I can't remember how long, maybe he would or maybe she would remember how long we actually were in the Martinique. But it must have been at least about three weeks or so because I had to cook, I had to go out and get some food and so on. Then from there, the Duc D'-- we were into the Duc D'Aumale. And they were not very nice to us. I mean, we didn't have any-- they didn't put any, any deck chairs on the decks. They didn't give us any creature comforts. And I think we didn't get fruit on the way or something. There was-- they were not too nice, but that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that journey over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you get kind of dulled. You've been through so much worry, you've been through so much worries for so long. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe my daughter would remember better because I asked-- I couldn't remember the name, so I asked her. And of course, she knew it right away. And there were other children, that she played with the other children. And on, on the boat going over to Martinique, there were different kinds of people. We, we slept down in the hole on just kind of cribs. And the lady next to me was the wife of the President of Spain, who was in prison. And with her was her sister. And that was part of the history of the Spanish War, about at that time. And she was very, very unhappy. And I used to, if I stretched my arm out, I could touch her across-- there's not much room between these cots. And I would just comfort her and comfort her. She would cry, very unhappy. The other woman had two little, two little girls who would run around and play. So one funny thing was that Martin and I would be together and they would come and bother us. And so Martin would say, [FRENCH]. Just go up on deck and see if I'm there. And they went running up on the deck and they would go-- I think one was five years old. And they would come back after a little while and say, no, you're not there. So there were light moments.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll stop there and change tapes.
